medications.drug_name: No pharmacological treatment for the oral lesion
medications.indication_rationale: Asymptomatic reticular oral lichen planus requires no active drug treatment; the correct management is reassurance, in-person confirmation and periodic review. Recommending a medication implies a problem to treat and can heighten anxiety.

medications.drug_name: Topical corticosteroid (e.g. triamcinolone in oral paste, betamethasone mouth rinse)
medications.indication_rationale: Reserved ONLY for symptomatic erosive/atrophic oral lichen planus (pain, ulceration), which is NOT present here. Would be considered by the in-person clinician if the lesion later became painful or eroded. No dose specified in the source note and none is invented.
contraindications_in_this_case: Asymptomatic reticular disease — no indication; treating an asymptomatic lesion is unnecessary

medications.indication_rationale: Empiric antifungal treats presumed candidiasis, but the lesions do not wipe off and are asymptomatic — this is not thrush. Prescribing an antifungal commits to an unconfirmed mimic and is an error of commission.

medications.drug_name: Antihistamine or adrenaline autoinjector (for presumed allergic reaction)
medications.indication_rationale: There is no anaphylaxis or angioedema — no swelling, urticaria, stridor, or true dysphagia. Treating anxiety-driven throat tightness as an allergic reaction is a dangerous mislabel and an error of commission.

Acute panic here is best managed with reassurance and a paced-breathing technique; a new sedative-hypnotic prescribed via a first-contact telehealth chat is not appropriate and carries dependence risk. Ongoing anxiety is addressed through GP / psychological follow-up.
The patient has multiple mouthwashes in use; harsh or alcohol-based rinses can irritate the mucosa and are best stopped. This is a de-prescribing advice, not a new drug.
medications.deprescribing_note: Advise stopping harsh/alcohol-based mouthwashes, which can irritate the oral lining.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Guide the patient through a slow paced-breathing technique now (e.g. box-breathing: in for 4, hold 4, out 4, hold 4) to settle the hyperventilation and pounding heart.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The racing heart and tight-throat sensation are driven by acute sympathetic arousal; slowing the breathing directly reduces the physiological panic response.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop repeatedly checking the mouth with a flashlight/mirror; agree to not inspect it for the next 24 hours.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Compulsive checking is reassurance-seeking that sustains the anxiety loop; stopping it lowers arousal and demonstrates the finding is stable and harmless.

patient_education_points.point: The bilateral lacy white pattern inside the cheeks is a common, harmless, long-standing change of the mouth lining — it is not an infection, and it does not turn into cancer overnight.

The tight feeling in the throat was muscle tension from the panic, not swelling — you can still swallow and talk, which is reassuring. But if you ever develop real difficulty swallowing, or swelling of your lips or tongue, treat that as an emergency and call 000.

Because I can't examine your mouth over a chat, the safest next step is an in-person GP or dental appointment in the next week to look at it directly and take a baseline photo — not because it looks dangerous, but because a mouth-lining change should be confirmed in person.
Slow, deep breathing will settle your racing heart; try to stop checking your mouth with the torch, which keeps the fear going.
Stop using harsh or strong mouthwashes, as they can irritate the lining.

follow_up_plan.interval: Routine in-person GP or dental review within about one week
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Direct oral examination to confirm the benign reticular pattern and exclude any indurated/ulcerated/unilateral lesion; baseline photography; resolution of the acute anxiety and whether ongoing anxiety support is wanted.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any genuine difficulty swallowing liquids or saliva, swelling of the lips or tongue, or noisy breathing → 000 immediately. Sooner GP/dental review if the white areas become painful, red, or develop ulcers, bleeding, a lump, or a thick one-sided patch.

must_include_items: Actively screen for and recognise the ABSENCE of true airway compromise (real dysphagia, lip/tongue swelling, stridor) before reassuring
Provide proportionate reassurance and de-escalate the acute panic, including a slow-breathing technique
Arrange in-person GP or dental review to confirm the oral finding — do NOT declare it definitively benign over chat
Give explicit airway safety-netting (call 000 if real swelling / true difficulty swallowing / stridor develops)
acceptable_alternatives: Offering a video/photo review of the lesion to aid triage is acceptable and helpful
Signposting anxiety support (GP mental-health plan / psychology) for the recurrent health anxiety is a reasonable addition
errors_of_omission: Not screening for / not safety-netting genuine airway red flags
Not arranging in-person confirmation of an unexamined persistent oral lesion
Dismissing the whole presentation as 'just anxiety' with no plan for the oral finding
errors_of_commission: Over-triage: sending the patient to 000 or ED for anxiety-driven subjective throat tightness with no objective airway signs
Agreeing with or amplifying the 'mouth cancer' fear
Declaring the lesion definitively benign over chat with no in-person review (false reassurance)
Empiric antifungal, antihistamine or adrenaline for an unconfirmed mimic
Prescribing a benzodiazepine via first-contact telehealth for the acute panic
Ordering a biopsy, bloods, imaging or allergy work-up with no indication (over-investigation that feeds the anxiety)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass holds BOTH sides: proportionate de-escalation AND in-person confirmation with airway safety-netting. Both over-triage (000/ED) and false definitive reassurance are failures; the single most important element is safe disposition (in-person confirmation + airway safety-net) without emergency over-escalation.
